tagline: Technology, culture, and the futures we keep rehearsing.

author.name: Mara Vale
author.title: Writer
author.bio: In Dust We Trust is Mara Vale's long-running notebook about technology, cities, art, AI, and the feeling that the future arrives as a texture before it arrives as policy. The voice is personal, observant, and a little suspicious of grand narratives.

homeIntro: A long-running notebook that starts in 2018 with desert optimism and follows the strange path from robot dreams to AI reality.

council.name: Rhea Coil
council.bio: Asks what the claim actually rests on, all the way down to observation.

council.name: Jonah Slate
council.bio: Wants the experiment, not the press release. If it can't be measured, it can't be defended.

council.name: Priya March
council.bio: Translates jargon into something a curious twelve-year-old could follow — and catches the hiding places of unexamined ideas.

council.name: Eli Rowan
council.bio: Looks for the unexpected mechanism, the anomaly that the main story ignores, the piece that doesn't fit.

topics: What would you need to believe for LLM hallucinations to be solvable — and what evidence would actually change that
The trouble with AI benchmarks: what we measure versus what we wish we measured
Reasoning in AI, explained without jargon — what is actually happening under the word 'thinking'
How to tell a real AI breakthrough from a branded one using only first principles
Teach yourself what scaling laws actually predict — and where they quietly stop
What chain-of-thought is, what it isn't, and why the distinction matters
The honest answer to 'does this model understand' and why that answer keeps moving
If you could only run one experiment on an LLM, which would teach you the most
Simplicity as a tell: when AI research hides complexity to look impressive
What AI evaluation can borrow from how physics treats anomalies
The puzzle of emergence: is capability jumping real or an artifact of measurement
Why AI agents fail the way children's first science projects fail — and what to do about it
Plain-English mechanics of attention: what the math is quietly doing
The difference between a model that memorizes and one that generalizes, told without equations
Why 'alignment' sounds like ethics but often reduces to an engineering discipline problem
What gets lost when AI demos replace AI experiments
How to read an AI paper the way Feynman would — checking the one experiment that would break it
The quiet epistemology inside every 'the model learned X' claim
Tool use in language models: what is actually a skill and what is just surface pattern
Why good AI criticism looks more like debugging than debate
homeNote: Start in 2018 if you want the origin story. Start in 2023 or 2026 if you want the archive at full speed. The thread is the same either way: what technology asks of the people living beside it.

startHere.note: The opening scene. Desert optimism, robots, dust, and the original question about what kind of life automation should serve.
A good middle-period entry, when the writing gets more social and less dazzled by the demo.
Where the archive starts sounding fully post-ChatGPT and openly skeptical of technological inevitability.
A compact statement of the newer mood: privacy, legibility, and keeping room for personhood.
The closing essay, and the clearest statement of what the whole notebook has been circling.
